comments:
Summary:
The paper presents a practical system for aligning recipe text to instructional cooking videos by combining three modalities: ASR-derived speech transcripts, parsed recipe text, and visual detectors trained with deep CNNs. The pipeline uses an input-output HMM to align recipe steps to transcript tokens, a keyword-spotting baseline and a hybrid method, and a visual refinement stage that shifts temporal windows to maximize visual detector scores. The authors construct a large corpus (≈180k recipe/video pairs and ≈1.4M labeled clips), evaluate clip quality with human raters, and demonstrate two applications (auto-illustration and within-video search).

Strengths:
- Clear motivation: procedural (multimodal) knowledge extraction is important and under-explored compared to declarative KBs.
- Practical, scalable system design combining complementary signals (text, speech, vision) that works at web scale.
- Large-scale corpus construction is a notable contribution — 180k aligned recipe-video pairs and 1.4M clips are useful resources for downstream work.
- Human evaluation is used to measure clip quality; comparisons to simple baselines (keyword spotting) are provided and show benefits of the hybrid/visual refinement approach.
- The paper describes useful downstream applications and provides qualitative examples.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Reliance on proprietary and noisy components: the ASR is noisy (acknowledged) and the large visual detector is trained on an internal 220M-image dataset with noisy labels; the paper does not provide quantitative evaluation of these detectors on a held-out, clean test set (beyond Food-101 finetuning). This limits reproducibility and makes it hard to attribute performance gains precisely to the visual component.
- HMM modeling assumptions are simplistic (strict left-to-right ordering, uniform prior α=K/T). While reasonable, further analysis of failure modes (e.g., repeated steps, out-of-order narration) is missing.
- Comparisons to prior work are brief. The evaluation compares mainly to keyword-spotting baselines; stronger baselines (e.g., other alignment models or multimodal fusion methods) are not empirically tested. The related work discussion cites relevant papers but does not include head-to-head experimental comparisons beyond small-scale prior datasets.
- Some key technical details are underspecified: exact feature/architecture details for affordance model (SVD rank, smoothing), hyperparameter selection, and more ablation (how much does each module — HMM, keyword-spotting, affordance, visual refinement — contribute separately?).
- The human evaluation uses a 3-point scale and reports averaged scores and confidence thresholds; confidence intervals, statistical significance testing, or per-action/object breakdowns would strengthen the claims.

Suggestions for improvement:
1) Add more quantitative ablation studies: show results for HMM-only, keyword-only, hybrid without vision, hybrid with vision, and the impact of the affordance reranking. Include precision/recall/F1 where possible, and per-class breakdowns for common actions/objects.
2) Provide clearer, reproducible descriptions of the affordance model (e.g., rank used for SVD, how probabilities are normalized), and of any thresholds used for filtering.
3) If possible, release a clean test split (or examples) and provide a quantitative evaluation of the visual detectors on held-out labeled data, or use an external benchmark to measure their accuracy. If training data is proprietary, provide a more detailed description of its noise level and composition.
4) Analyze failure cases qualitatively: when does the HMM fail (ASR errors, out-of-order narration), and when does visual refinement hurt (objects visually ambiguous or occluded)?
5) Consider more sophisticated temporal models (e.g., semi-Markov or duration models) or models that allow steps to be revisited, and discuss limitations clearly.
6) Add statistical significance tests for human evaluation comparisons and show inter-rater agreement per task type.

Conclusion:
This is a well-motivated, practically useful paper that brings together multiple modalities to align textual instructions and videos at scale. The main scientific contributions are the hybrid alignment pipeline and the resulting large-scale corpora. The paper would be strengthened by more rigorous quantitative analyses, clearer specification of certain components, and stronger reproducibility details. Nonetheless, the work is of clear interest to the multimodal/vision-and-language community and provides a valuable dataset and methodology.
